explanation: The natural language statement "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" implies that the system must always allow the user to switch the state of the leak compensation feature between enabled and disabled. The 'at any time' suggests a global property. The LTL formula captures this by stating: 1. If leak compensation is currently enabled (`enableLeakCompensation`), it must always be eventually possible to disable it (`F(!enableLeakCompensation)`). 2. If leak compensation is currently disabled (`!enableLeakCompensation`), it must always be eventually possible to enable it (`F(enableLeakCompensation)`). The `G` operator ensures that these conditions hold true globally, reflecting the 'at any time' requirement for the user's ability to control the feature.

The LTL formula captures the requirement that the user is able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time. The "at any time" is represented by the global operator G. The first part, (leakCompensationActive -> F !leakCompensationActive), states that if the leak compensation feature is currently active, it is always possible for it to eventually become inactive, which represents the user's ability to disable it. The second part, (!leakCompensationActive -> F leakCompensationActive), states that if the leak compensation feature is currently inactive, it is always possible for it to eventually become active, which represents the user's ability to enable it. Together, these two implications ensure that the system never gets stuck in a state where the user cannot change the status of the leak compensation feature, thus confirming the user's continuous ability to toggle it.

The natural language statement implies two main capabilities for the user regarding the leak compensation feature: enabling and disabling it at any point in time. "At any time" translates to the global operator (G). For the 'enable' part, the property states that if the user performs the action to enable leak compensation (`enableLeakCompensation`), then eventually the feature will become active (`leakCompensationActive`). This is expressed as G (enableLeakCompensation -> F leakCompensationActive). For the 'disable' part, since there is no explicit atomic proposition for a 'disable' action, the most suitable translation in LTL is to ensure that the system does not get permanently stuck in the `leakCompensationActive` state. This means that if leak compensation is active, it must eventually become inactive, thereby reflecting the user's ability to turn it off. This is captured by G (leakCompensationActive -> F !leakCompensationActive). While this formulation implies it *will* eventually be disabled rather than just *can* be, it is the strongest LTL guarantee that ensures the system is not preventing the feature from being turned off, thus fulfilling the 'able to disable' requirement.

The natural language statement "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" indicates that the user must always have the capability to switch the state of the leak compensation feature. The atomic proposition "enableLeakCompensation" is interpreted as the state where the leak compensation feature is enabled by the user. Therefore, the property must ensure two things globally: 1) If the feature is currently enabled (enableLeakCompensation), it must be possible to eventually disable it (F !enableLeakCompensation). 2) If the feature is currently disabled (!enableLeakCompensation), it must be possible to eventually enable it (F enableLeakCompensation). The "at any time" part is captured by the global operator (G) encompassing these two conditional eventualities. The conjunction (&) ensures both conditions always hold.

The natural language statement specifies that the user must always have the ability to either enable or disable the leak compensation feature. 'At any time' translates to a global requirement (G) meaning that from any state, this capability must hold. 'Shall be able to' implies that it is always eventually possible (F) to perform the action or reach the desired state. The atomic proposition 'enableLeakCompensation' indicates that the user enables the leak compensation feature. Assuming this proposition represents the state of the user's setting for this feature (true when enabled, false when disabled), then 'enableLeakCompensation' covers the 'enable' aspect and '!enableLeakCompensation' covers the 'disable' aspect. Therefore, 'G F enableLeakCompensation' means that at any point, the user can eventually enable the feature, and 'G F !enableLeakCompensation' means that at any point, the user can eventually disable it. Combining these with a logical AND ensures both capabilities are continuously present.

The natural language statement "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" indicates that the user must always have the control to switch the state of the leak compensation feature. The atomic proposition `enableLeakCompensation` signifies that the user has enabled the leak compensation. Its negation, `!enableLeakCompensation`, logically represents the state where the feature is disabled or not enabled by the user. "At any time" translates to a global property, meaning this ability holds throughout the system's operation. The property is composed of two parts joined by a logical AND:
1. `G (enableLeakCompensation -> F !enableLeakCompensation)`: This part states that if the leak compensation is currently enabled (`enableLeakCompensation`), then it is always (globally `G`) eventually possible (`F`) for it to become disabled (`!enableLeakCompensation`). This captures the user's ability to disable the feature.
2. `G (!enableLeakCompensation -> F enableLeakCompensation)`: This part states that if the leak compensation is currently disabled (`!enableLeakCompensation`), then it is always (globally `G`) eventually possible (`F`) for it to become enabled (`enableLeakCompensation`). This captures the user's ability to enable the feature.

Combining these two ensures that regardless of the current state of the leak compensation feature (enabled or disabled), the system always allows for a future state where the opposite is true, thus guaranteeing the user's continuous ability to toggle the feature.

The natural language statement "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" implies that the system must always allow the `enableLeakCompensation` state to change. This means that if the leak compensation is currently enabled (`enableLeakCompensation`), it must be possible to eventually disable it (F !`enableLeakCompensation`). Conversely, if the leak compensation is currently disabled (`!enableLeakCompensation`), it must be possible to eventually enable it (F `enableLeakCompensation`). The 'at any time' part is captured by the global operator (G), ensuring this capability holds throughout the system's operation. Thus, the LTL property `G ((enableLeakCompensation -> F !enableLeakCompensation) & (!enableLeakCompensation -> F enableLeakCompensation))` precisely states that the leak compensation feature is always switchable, preventing it from getting stuck in either an enabled or disabled state.

The statement "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" implies two key aspects: (1) the user always has the option to perform the action of enabling or disabling the feature, and (2) performing this action correctly toggles the state of the leak compensation feature. Since there is a single atomic proposition 'enableLeakCompensation' (indicating user enables leak compensation) and no explicit 'disableLeakCompensation' proposition, 'enableLeakCompensation' is interpreted as a toggle action. The LTL property is therefore composed of two parts connected by an 'AND' operator:1. 'G(X enableLeakCompensation)': This captures the 'user shall be able to ... at any time' part. It asserts that at any given time (G, Globally), there exists a possible next state (X) where the 'enableLeakCompensation' action (interpreted as a toggle) occurs. This ensures that the user always has the choice and the system allows this action to be initiated.2. 'G(enableLeakCompensation -> ((leakCompensationActive -> X !leakCompensationActive) & (!leakCompensationActive -> X leakCompensationActive)))': This part defines the effect of the 'enableLeakCompensation' action. It states that if the 'enableLeakCompensation' action occurs (user toggles the feature), then in the very next state (X), the 'leakCompensationActive' state will flip its value. Specifically, if it was active, it becomes inactive (leakCompensationActive -> X !leakCompensationActive), and if it was inactive, it becomes active (!leakCompensationActive -> X leakCompensationActive).Combining these two parts ensures both the continuous availability of the toggle action for the user and the correct behavioral response of the system to that action.
output_LTL: G(X enableLeakCompensation) & G(enableLeakCompensation -> ((leakCompensationActive -> X !leakCompensationActive) & (!leakCompensationActive -> X leakCompensationActive)))

explanation: The natural language statement "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" implies two main conditions that must always hold. Firstly, from any point in time, it must be eventually possible for the user to enable the leak compensation feature. This is captured by the LTL formula G(F enableLeakCompensation), meaning 'Globally, it is eventually true that the user enables leak compensation'. Secondly, from any point in time, it must be eventually possible for the user to disable the leak compensation feature. Since there isn't a direct 'disableLeakCompensation' atomic proposition, we use the negation of 'enableLeakCompensation' to represent the state where the user is not enabling (i.e., implicitly disabling or having disabled) the feature. This is captured by G(F !enableLeakCompensation), meaning 'Globally, it is eventually true that the user does not enable leak compensation'. The conjunction (AND) of these two properties ensures that the ability to switch between enabled and disabled states is continuously available at any time.

The natural language statement "The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time" implies a liveness property. It means that regardless of the current state of the leak compensation feature (enabled or disabled), the user always has the capability to change its state. Therefore, if the leak compensation is currently disabled (`!enableLeakCompensation`), it must be possible to eventually enable it (`F enableLeakCompensation`). Conversely, if the leak compensation is currently enabled (`enableLeakCompensation`), it must be possible to eventually disable it (`F !enableLeakCompensation`). The phrase "at any time" translates to the global operator (G), ensuring this property holds continuously.
